questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: An alternating current is given by the equation i = i<sub>1</sub> sin $$\omega$$t + i<sub>2</sub> cos $$\omega$$t. The rms current will be :

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A power transmission line feeds input power at $$2.3 \mathrm{~kV}$$ to a step down transformer with its primary winding having 3000 turns. The output power is delivered at $$230 \mathrm{~V}$$ by the transformer. The current in the primary of the transformer is $$5 \mathrm{~A}$$ and its efficiency is $$90 \%$$. The winding of transformer is made of copper. The output current of transformer is _________ $$A$$.
questions.solution: $$\begin{aligned} & P_i=2300 \times 5 \text { watt } \\ & P_0=2300 \times 5 \times 0.9=230 \times I_2 \\ & I_2=45 A \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText:
The alternating current is given by $$i = \left\{ {\sqrt {42} \sin \left( {{{2\pi } \over T}t} \right) + 10} \right\}A$$

The r.m.s. value of of this current is ................. A.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A 15 $$\Omega$$ resistor, an 80 mH inductor and a capacitor of capacitance C are connected in series with a 50 Hz ac source. If the source voltage and current in the circuit are in phase, then the value of capacitance is
questions.solution:
When the voltage and current are in phase in an RLC series circuit, the circuit is at resonance. At resonance, the inductive reactance ($$X_L$$) and capacitive reactance ($$X_C$$) are equal, i.e., $$X_L = X_C$$.

The inductive reactance $$X_L$$ is given by:

$$ X_L = 2\pi f L $$

where $$f$$ is the frequency and $$L$$ is the inductance. Here, $$f = 50$$ Hz and $$L = 80$$ mH = 0.08 H.

Substituting the values:

$$ X_L = 2\pi \times 50 \times 0.08 = 25.12 \ \Omega $$

The capacitive reactance $$X_C$$ is given by:

$$ X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C} $$

At resonance, $$X_L = X_C$$, so:

$$ 25.12 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times C} $$

Solving for $$C$$:

$$ C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 25.12} $$

Calculating the value of $$C$$:

$$ C \approx 1.27 \times 10^{-4} \ F = 127 \ \mu F $$

Hence, the value of the capacitance is 127 $$\mu$$F, which corresponds to Option B.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: Two circuits are shown in the figure (a) & (b). At a frequency of ____________ rad/s the average power dissipated in one cycle will be same in both the circuits.
questions.solution:
For figure (a)

$${P_{avg}} = {{v_{rms}^2} \over R}$$

$${{v_{rms}^2} \over {{Z^2}}} \times R = {{v_{rms}^2} \over R} \times 1$$

$${R^2} = {Z^2}$$

$$25 = {\left( {\sqrt {{{({x_C} - {x_L})}^2} + {5^2}} } \right)^2}$$

$$ = 25{({x_C} - {x_L})^2} + 25$$

$${x_C} = {x_L} \Rightarrow {1 \over {\omega C}} = \omega L$$

$${\omega ^2} = {1 \over {LC}} = {{{{10}^6}} \over {0.1 \times 40}}$$

$$\omega = 500$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: The core of any transformer is laminated so as  to
options: reduce the energy loss due to eddy currents
questions.solution: Laminated core provide less area of cross-section for the current to flow. Because of this, resistance  of the core increases and current decreases thereby decreasing the eddy current losses.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A capacitor C is first charged fully with potential difference of $V_0$ and disconnected from the battery. The charged capacitor is connected across an inductor having inductance L. In $t$ s, 25% of the initial energy in the capacitor is transferred to the inductor. The value of $t$ is ________ s.
questions.solution:
When a charged capacitor is connected to an inductor, the energy oscillates between the electric field of the capacitor and the magnetic field of the inductor.

The charge q on the capacitor at any time t is given by :

$$ \mathrm{q}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{Q}_0 \cos (\omega \mathrm{t}) $$

Where :

  • $\mathrm{Q}_0$ is the initial maximum charge.

  • $\omega$ is the angular frequency, defined as $\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}$.

Initially all energy is in the capacitor.

$$ \mathrm{U}_0=\frac{\mathrm{Q}_0^2}{2 \mathrm{C}} $$

Energy in capacitor at time t is :

$$ U_C(t)=\frac{q(t)^2}{2 C}=\frac{\left(Q_0 \cos (\omega t)\right)^2}{2 C}=U_0 \cos ^2(\omega t) $$

By conservation of energy the energy stored in inductor as magnetic field,

$$\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{U}_0-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{C}} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{L}}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{U}_0\left(1-\cos ^2(\omega \mathrm{t})\right)=\mathrm{U}_0 \sin ^2(\omega \mathrm{t})$$

It is that $25 \%$ of the initial energy is transferred to the inductor :

$$ \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{U}_0 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $U_0 \sin ^2(\omega t)=\frac{1}{4} U_0$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $\sin ^2(\omega \mathrm{t})=\frac{1}{4} $

$$\Rightarrow $$$ \sin (\omega \mathrm{t})=\frac{1}{2} $

$$\Rightarrow $$$ \omega \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi}{6} $

$$\Rightarrow $$$ \frac{\mathrm{t}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}=\frac{\pi}{6} $

$$\Rightarrow $$ $ \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi \sqrt{\mathrm{LC}}}{6}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A circuit is connected to an ac source of variable frequency. As the frequency of the source is increased, the current first increases and then decreases. Which of the following combinations of elements is likely to comprise the circuit?
questions.solution:
This behavior is indicative of a resonance phenomenon in an AC circuit. Resonance occurs when the inductive reactance ($$X_L$$) and the capacitive reactance ($$X_C$$) are equal, causing the impedance of the circuit to be at a minimum value and thus the current to be at a maximum.

The impedance of an RLC circuit can be written as:

$$ Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2} $$

Here, $$X_L = \omega L$$ and $$X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$, where $$\omega$$ is the angular frequency of the source. At resonance, $$X_L = X_C$$, meaning:

$$ \omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C} $$

Solving for $$\omega$$, we find the resonant frequency:

$$ \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} $$

At this frequency, the impedance $$Z$$ is minimized, allowing for the maximum current. As the frequency moves away from this resonant frequency, the impedance of the circuit increases, and the current decreases.

This characteristic pattern of increasing and then decreasing current with increasing frequency suggests that the circuit must contain all three elements: an inductor (L), a capacitor (C), and a resistor (R). Therefore, the correct combination of elements is found in:

Option A: L, C, and R

questions.questionText: An alternating emf $$\mathrm{E}=440 \sin 100 \pi \mathrm{t}$$ is applied to a circuit containing an inductance of $$\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \mathrm{H}$$. If an a.c. ammeter is connected in the circuit, its reading will be :

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A circuit has a resistance of $$12$$ $$ohm$$ and an impedance of $$15$$ $$ohm$$. The power factor of the circuit will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText:
The equation of current in a purely inductive circuit is $$5 \sin \left(49\, \pi t-30^{\circ}\right)$$. If the inductance is $$30 \,\mathrm{mH}$$ then the equation for the voltage across the inductor, will be :

$$\left\{\right.$$ Let $$\left.\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right\}$$
questions.solution:
$$V(t) = I\omega L\sin (49\pi t - 30^\circ + 90^\circ )$$

$$ = 5 \times 49\pi \times {{30} \over {1000}}\sin (49\pi t + 60^\circ )$$

$$ = 23.1\sin (49\pi t + 60^\circ )$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A voltage $v=v_0 \sin \omega t$ applied to a circuit drives a current $i=i_0 \sin (\omega t+\phi)$ in the circuit. The average power consumed in the circuit over a cycle is
questions.solution:
$v=v_0 \sin \omega t$ and $i=i_0 \sin (\omega t+\phi)$

Average power over a full cycle is

$$ \begin{aligned} P_{a v} & =v_{\mathrm{rms}} i_{\mathrm{rms}} \cos \phi \\ & =\frac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{i_0}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi \\ \therefore \quad P_{a v} & =\frac{v_0 i_0}{2} \cos \phi \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: In series LCR circuit, the capacitance is changed from $C$ to $4 C$. To keep the resonance frequency unchanged, the new inductance should be:
questions.solution:
The resonance frequency $$ f_0 $$ of an LCR circuit is given by:

$$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} $$

where:

  • $$ L $$ is the inductance.
  • $$ C $$ is the capacitance.

To keep the resonance frequency unchanged when the capacitance is changed from $$ C $$ to $$ 4C $$, we must adjust the inductance $$ L $$ to a new value $$ L' $$ such that:

$$ \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L' \cdot 4C}} $$

Now solving for $$ L' $$:

$$ \sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{4CL'} $$

Squaring both sides, we have:

$$ LC = 4CL' $$

Dividing both sides by $$ 4C $$, we get:

$$ \frac{L}{4} = L' $$

Thus, the new inductance $$ L' $$ is one fourth of the original inductance $$ L $$. Therefore, to achieve the same resonance frequency with the capacitance increased to $$ 4C $$, the inductance should be reduced to a quarter of its initial value:

$$ L' = \frac{L}{4} $$

This means we have reduced the inductance by $$ \frac{3}{4}L $$, so the correct answer is:

Option C: reduced by $$ \frac{3}{4}L $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A 100$$\Omega$$ resistance, a 0.1 $$\mu$$F capacitor and an inductor are connected in series across a 250 V supply at variable frequency. Calculate the value of inductance of inductor at which resonance will occur. Given that the resonant frequency is 60 Hz.
questions.solution:
C = 0.1 $$\mu$$F = 10$$-$$7 F

Resonant frequency = 60 Hz.

$${\omega _0} = {1 \over {\sqrt {LC} }}$$

$$2\pi {f_0} = {1 \over {\sqrt {LC} }} \Rightarrow L = {1 \over {4{\pi ^2}f_0^2C}}$$

by putting values $$L \simeq 70.3$$ Hz.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A resistance $$R$$ and inductance $$L$$ and a capacitor $$C$$ all are connected in series with an $$\mathrm{AC}$$ supply. The resistance of $$R$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$ and for a given frequency, the inductive reactance of $$L$$ is 36 ohm and capacitive reactance of $$C$$ is $$24 \mathrm{~ohm}$$. If the current in the circuit is $$5 \mathrm{~amp}$$. Find the potential difference across $$R, L$$ and $$C$$.
questions.solution:
To determine the potential differences across the resistor $$R$$, inductor $$L$$, and capacitor $$C$$ in a series AC circuit, we can use Ohm's Law and the given impedances.

Given:

  • $$R = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Inductive reactance $$X_L = 36 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Capacitive reactance $$X_C = 24 \textrm{ ohm}$$

  • Current in the circuit $$I = 5 \textrm{ amp}$$

First, we find the potential difference across each component using Ohm's Law:

  1. Potential difference across $$R$$:

$$ V_R = I \cdot R $$

$$ V_R = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_R = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$L$$:

$$ V_L = I \cdot X_L $$

$$ V_L = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 36 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_L = 180 \textrm{ V} $$

  1. Potential difference across $$C$$:

$$ V_C = I \cdot X_C $$

$$ V_C = 5 \textrm{ amp} \cdot 24 \textrm{ ohm} $$

$$ V_C = 120 \textrm{ V} $$

Summarizing, the potential differences are:

  • $$V_R = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_L = 180 \textrm{ V}$$

  • $$V_C = 120 \textrm{ V}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option D: $$120, 180, 120 \textrm{ V}$$
